So the code would be:

- Initialize an empty stack.
- Iterate through each character in the input string.
- For each character, check if the stack is not empty and the top forms a consecutive pair with the current character.
- If so, pop the top and do not push the current character.
- Else, push the current character onto the stack.
- Finally, construct the result string from the stack.

So the code in C++ would be:

#include <stack>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    string resultingString(string s) {
        stack<char> stk;
        for (char c : s) {
            if (!stk.empty()) {
                char top = stk.top();
                if ( (abs(c - top) == 1) || ( (c == 'a' && top == 'z') || (c == 'z' && top == 'a') ) ) {
                    stk.pop();
                    continue;
                }
            }
            stk.push(c);
        }
        string result;
        while (!stk.empty()) {
            result.push_back(stk.top());
            stk.pop();
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
#include <stack>
#include <algorithm>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    string resultingString(string s) {
        stack<char> stk;
        for (char c : s) {
            if (!stk.empty()) {
                char top = stk.top();
                if ((abs(c - top) == 1) || (c == 'a' && top == 'z') || (c == 'z' && top == 'a')) {
                    stk.pop();
                    continue;
                }
            }
            stk.push(c);
        }
        string result;
        while (!stk.empty()) {
            result += stk.top();
            stk.pop();
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a stack to efficiently remove consecutive character pairs in a single pass through the string. Each character is pushed onto the stack, and if it forms a consecutive pair with the top of the stack, both are removed. This approach ensures that each character is processed a constant number of times, leading to linear time complexity. The space complexity is also linear in the worst case, where no pairs are removed. The energy implications are low due to the efficient processing and minimal memory operations.
